skill.name: markitdown
skill.description: Convert documents, media, web pages, and structured files to Markdown using Microsoft MarkItDown workflows and helper scripts.
skill.summary: Convert PDFs, Office files, images, audio, web pages, and data files into LLM-ready Markdown.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, credential, and weak-crypto patterns. Most command and weak-crypto hits are documentation examples or Markdown variable names, but the skill legitimately reads local files, writes outputs, can load plugins, and can send document-derived content to OpenRouter when AI features are enabled.

medium_findings.title: Optional External AI Processing Can Disclose Document Content
medium_findings.description: The AI conversion helper initializes an OpenRouter client and converts local files with an LLM-backed MarkItDown instance. When users enable this path, images or document-derived content may be sent to an external model provider.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script directly configures an external OpenRouter API endpoint and passes it into MarkItDown for conversion. Use appears intentional and user configured, but it is a real data disclosure path.

medium_findings.title: Broad Local File Read and Write Workflows
medium_findings.description: Batch and literature scripts enumerate input directories, convert matching files, create directories, and write Markdown, index, and catalog outputs. This is expected for the skill, but users should run it only on intended directories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scripts clearly enumerate files and write generated outputs. This is legitimate conversion behavior, but it confirms filesystem access beyond a single fixed file.

medium_findings.title: Optional Third-Party Plugin Loading
medium_findings.description: The batch conversion script exposes a plugin flag that enables MarkItDown plugins. Third-party plugins can expand behavior beyond the reviewed files and require separate trust decisions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed script only toggles MarkItDown plugin support, so risk depends on installed plugins. The behavior is explicit and user controlled.

low_findings.title: Shell Command Detections Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Many external command hits are fenced Markdown examples for installing packages or running conversion commands. They do not execute automatically, but users should review commands before running them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited items are displayed as setup or usage snippets in Markdown. No reviewed file contains code that automatically runs these shell commands.

low_findings.title: Placeholder API Keys And Environment Access
low_findings.description: Credential hits are mostly placeholder OpenRouter key examples and an environment variable lookup. No hardcoded live secret was found, but users should avoid saving real keys in shell profile files on shared systems.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples use placeholder key formats and the script reads a user-provided environment variable. There is no evidence of embedded valid credentials.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Hits Are Markdown Naming False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections appear to match Markdown-related names such as md variables and .md outputs. No evidence of MD5, SHA1, or custom cryptography was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Searches found no MD5, SHA1, hashlib, cipher, or comparable cryptographic implementation. The affected files use Markdown abbreviations and model examples.

dangerous_patterns.title: External LLM Endpoint Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: OpenRouter base URL and API-key driven client setup allow optional network processing of converted document content.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint and API key are explicit. The risk is contextual because users must opt in with credentials.

dangerous_patterns.title: Directory Enumeration With Output Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: Scripts glob or recursively search directories and write generated Markdown outputs to user-specified locations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Directory traversal and writes are directly present and match the conversion purpose. No hidden destructive behavior was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Enabled Plugin Expansion
dangerous_patterns.description: The --plugins flag enables installed MarkItDown plugins, which can introduce behavior outside this reviewed skill package.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The flag is explicit and reviewed code does not install plugins. Risk depends on the user environment and installed plugin set.

content.value_statement: Mixed document collections are hard to prepare for AI review and search. This skill converts common files, media, and web sources into structured Markdown workflows.
actual_capabilities: Convert PDF, DOCX, PPTX, XLSX, HTML, CSV, JSON, XML, EPUB, ZIP, and media files to Markdown.
Use OCR and transcription features when the needed optional MarkItDown dependencies are installed.
Batch convert files from directories with optional recursive search and worker control.
Convert scientific papers and create Markdown indexes and catalogs for literature review.
Add AI image descriptions through OpenRouter when users provide an API key.
Document CLI, Python API, format-specific, and OpenRouter integration workflows.
limitations: Format support depends on installed MarkItDown extras and system tools.
AI-enhanced descriptions can send document-derived content to OpenRouter.
OCR and transcription quality depend on source quality and local dependencies.
Plugin-enabled conversion can depend on unreviewed third-party plugins.

use_cases.title: Prepare Literature Reviews
use_cases.description: Convert paper collections to Markdown, organize outputs by year, and create indexes for review workflows.

use_cases.title: Migrate Office Content
use_cases.description: Transform Word, PowerPoint, spreadsheet, and HTML sources into Markdown for documentation systems.

use_cases.title: Extract Structured Source Material
use_cases.description: Convert tables, CSV, JSON, XML, images, and transcripts into text that AI tools can inspect.

prompt_templates.title: Convert One File
prompt_templates.prompt: Use markitdown to convert {input_file} into Markdown and save the result as {output_file}.

prompt_templates.title: Batch Convert Folder
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert all {file_types} files in {input_folder} to Markdown in {output_folder}. Use recursive search only if needed.

prompt_templates.title: Build Literature Index
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert the PDFs in {papers_folder} to Markdown, organize them by year, and create an index for review.

prompt_templates.title: Add AI Image Descriptions
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert {input_file} with AI image descriptions using OpenRouter. Warn me before sending sensitive content externally.

output_examples.input: Convert a research paper PDF to Markdown.
output: Created a Markdown file with the paper title and extracted sections.
Preserved readable headings, lists, and tables where conversion supported them.
Reported the source filename and output location.

output_examples.input: Batch convert a folder of Word and PowerPoint files.
output: Converted each matching file into a separate Markdown document.
Skipped unsupported files and summarized conversion success and failures.
Saved all outputs in the requested directory.

output_examples.input: Convert slides with AI image descriptions.
output: Generated Markdown slide content with descriptions for visual elements.
Included the selected AI model and prompt type in the output header.
Used the configured OpenRouter key for the external vision step.

best_practices: Install only the MarkItDown extras needed for your file formats.
Use a dedicated output folder so converted files do not mix with sources.
Review sensitive files before enabling OpenRouter, Azure, YouTube, or plugin workflows.
anti_patterns: Do not run recursive conversion over broad home or shared directories.
Do not paste API keys into prompts, documents, or committed files.
Do not enable third-party plugins unless you trust their source and behavior.

faq.question: What does this skill convert?
faq.answer: It documents MarkItDown workflows for PDFs, Office files, images, audio, web pages, structured data, archives, EPUBs, and YouTube transcripts.

faq.question: Does conversion run locally?
faq.answer: Basic conversion is local. AI image descriptions, Azure Document Intelligence, and YouTube transcript workflows can contact external services.

faq.question: Do I need extra dependencies?
faq.answer: Yes for some formats. Install the specific MarkItDown extras and system tools required for your document types.

faq.question: Can it process many files at once?
faq.answer: Yes. The batch script can process directories, filter extensions, search recursively, and use multiple workers.

faq.question: How are API keys handled?
faq.answer: The AI helper reads an OpenRouter key from a command option or environment variable. It does not include a real key.

faq.question: Is it safe for confidential documents?
faq.answer: Use local conversion for confidential files. Avoid external AI, Azure, YouTube, and unreviewed plugins unless disclosure is approved.
